questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with central chest pain radiating to the back, dyspnoea, and hypotension. On examination, heart sounds are muffled, JVP is raised, and trachea is central. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute myocardial infarction with cardiogenic shock
questions.explanation: Muffled heart sounds, raised JVP, and hypotension are classic for cardiac tamponade (Beck's triad). Tension pneumothorax would cause tracheal deviation and absent breath sounds. Massive PE would show signs of right heart strain without muffled heart sounds. Acute MI with cardiogenic shock usually has pulmonary oedema. Aortic dissection often presents with pulse deficit or neurological signs.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a one-week history of progressive fatigue, weight loss, nausea, and dizziness on standing. She has hyperpigmentation on her knuckles and buccal mucosa. BP 85/50 mmHg supine, HR 110 bpm. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Hyperpigmentation is pathognomonic for Addison's disease due to increased ACTH. Septic shock would have fever and a source of infection. Hypovolaemic shock would have a clear history of fluid loss. Anaphylaxis is associated with a trigger, urticaria, and wheeze. Cardiogenic shock presents with pulmonary oedema and raised JVP.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with known alcoholic liver disease presents with haematemesis and melaena. He is hypotensive (BP 80/50 mmHg), tachycardic, and has splenomegaly. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Alcoholic liver disease with splenomegaly indicates portal hypertension, making variceal bleeding the most likely cause of upper GI haemorrhage. Peptic ulcer is possible but less common without NSAID history. Mallory-Weiss tear follows retching. Gastric cancer presents with weight loss and anorexia. Angiodysplasia is more common in the right colon and rarely causes massive haematemesis.

questions.stem: A 34-week pregnant woman suddenly becomes dizzy and hypotensive (BP 75/50 mmHg) while lying supine on the examination table. She has no vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, or respiratory distress. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Supine hypotensive syndrome occurs when the gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava, reducing venous return. It is relieved by turning the patient on the left side. Placental abruption causes abdominal pain and vaginal bleeding. Uterine rupture presents with severe pain and fetal distress. Amniotic fluid embolism causes sudden hypoxia and cardiovascular collapse. Massive PE causes chest pain and hypoxia.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old man is found on the floor after a fall from a stepladder. He is bradycardic (HR 50 bpm) and hypotensive (BP 70/40 mmHg). His skin is warm and dry. There is no obvious external bleeding or rash. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bradycardia with hypotension after suspected spinal injury is characteristic of neurogenic shock due to loss of sympathetic tone. Septic, hypovolaemic, and anaphylactic shocks typically cause tachycardia. Cardiogenic shock would show pulmonary oedema and raised JVP. The warm, dry skin is also consistent with loss of vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with fever, cough, and confusion. BP 85/50, HR 110, RR 28, SpO2 91% on air. Temperature 38.9°C. Capillary refill 4 seconds. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis of sepsis?
questions.explanation: Lactate is the key diagnostic marker for sepsis-induced tissue hypoperfusion, with a level >2 mmol/L indicating sepsis. Blood cultures identify the pathogen but do not confirm the diagnosis of sepsis itself; CXR and CT chest identify a source but not the systemic condition.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents with haematemesis and melaena. BP 90/60, HR 115. She is pale. What is the single most appropriate investigation to establish the diagnosis of the source of bleeding?
questions.explanation: OGD is the gold standard to directly visualise and identify the source of upper GI bleeding. FBC may show anaemia but does not confirm the source; U&Es and coagulation screen are supportive but not diagnostic; abdominal X-ray is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man is brought to the ED after a road traffic accident. He is hypotensive (BP 80/50) with a distended abdomen. What is the most appropriate investigation to identify the cause of hypotension?
options: Extended Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma (eFAST)
questions.explanation: eFAST is the first-line investigation in unstable trauma patients to detect intra-abdominal free fluid (haemorrhage). CT requires patient stability and contrast; DPL is more invasive; abdominal X-ray is not sensitive for haemoperitoneum; focused abdominal ultrasound without the extended views may miss pericardial or thoracic blood.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known asthma presents with acute dyspnoea, wheeze, and hypotension after eating peanuts. She has urticaria and angioedema. What is the most appropriate test to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Serum tryptase is elevated during anaphylaxis within 1-2 hours, confirming mast cell degranulation. Skin prick and specific IgE tests identify the allergen but are not acute diagnostic tests; C1 esterase inhibitor relates to hereditary angioedema; PEF may show airflow obstruction but is not diagnostic of anaphylaxis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 8 weeks gestation presents with acute lower abdominal pain, syncope, and hypotension. On examination, she has cervical excitation and adnexal tenderness. What is the single most appropriate test to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound can visualize an ectopic pregnancy and free fluid in the pelvis, making it the first-line diagnostic test in a haemodynamically unstable pregnant patient. Urine pregnancy test is positive but does not locate the pregnancy; serial beta-hCG can guide but is not definitive acutely; CT is contraindicated in pregnancy; laparoscopy is invasive and typically reserved if ultrasound is equivocal.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman develops sudden-onset urticaria, stridor, and hypotension 10 minutes after receiving intravenous cefuroxime. She has a known penicillin allergy. What is the single most appropriate first-line drug therapy?
questions.explanation: In anaphylaxis, intramuscular adrenaline (0.5 mg 1:1000) is the first-line treatment. Intravenous adrenaline is only used in specialist settings for refractory shock due to risk of arrhythmia. Antihistamines and steroids are second-line.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with fever, confusion, and hypotension (BP 80/40 mmHg). Lactate is 4.5 mmol/L. He has been started on intravenous fluids (1 litre crystalloid) with no improvement. What is the most appropriate next vasopressor therapy?
questions.explanation: In septic shock refractory to fluids, noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor (Surviving Sepsis Campaign). Dopamine is no longer preferred due to arrhythmogenicity. Adrenaline is second-line, and vasopressin is an add-on. Dobutamine is indicated for low cardiac output, not first-line.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old male is brought to the emergency department with severe central chest pain and hypotension (BP 85/50 mmHg). ECG shows anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction. He has pulmonary oedema on examination. What is the most appropriate first-line inotropic therapy?
questions.explanation: In cardiogenic shock with low cardiac output, dobutamine is the first-line inotrope (NICE guideline CG152). Noradrenaline is for vasodilatory shock. Milrinone and levosimendan are second-line. Dopamine has higher arrhythmogenicity and is not preferred.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is brought in after a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He is hypotensive (BP 70/40 mmHg), tachycardic, and has a distended abdomen. Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma (FAST) shows free intra-abdominal fluid. He has already received 2 litres of crystalloid with transient improvement. What is the most appropriate immediate therapy?
options: Administer 4 units of packed red blood cells (group O negative)
Insert a chest drain for possible haemothorax
Transfer to CT scan for further imaging
questions.explanation: In haemorrhagic shock with evidence of ongoing bleeding, the first-line therapy is blood product transfusion (packed red cells) to restore oxygen-carrying capacity. Crystalloid is insufficient. Tranexamic acid is given if <3 hours from injury but not primary therapy. Immediate transfer to CT is inappropriate in unstable patient; surgery is definitive but blood should be given first.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known Addison's disease presents with vomiting, hypotension (BP 85/50 mmHg), and hyponatraemia. She is unable to take oral medications. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: In adrenal crisis, the first-line treatment is intravenous hydrocortisone (100 mg bolus) to provide both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity, along with fluid resuscitation. Intramuscular route is slower. Dexamethasone lacks mineralocorticoid effect and is not first-line. Fludrocortisone is for maintenance, not acute crisis.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents to the ED with fever, dysuria, and confusion. Observations: BP 85/50, HR 115, RR 24, SpO2 97% on air, temp 39.2°C. Capillary refill 4 seconds. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Give an IV crystalloid fluid bolus (e.g., 500 mL Hartmann's)
Insert a central venous catheter for monitoring
Repeat the blood pressure measurement in 5 minutes
Start IV broad-spectrum antibiotics after taking blood cultures
Request an urgent CT abdomen and pelvis
questions.explanation: In septic shock (hypotension with signs of hypoperfusion), the immediate priority is to start fluid resuscitation with crystalloid, as per the Surviving Sepsis Campaign and NICE guidelines. Antibiotics should follow after cultures, but fluid is given first. Repositioning or waiting is inappropriate. Central line insertion is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known alcoholic cirrhosis and previous variceal bleeds presents with haematemesis and melaena. He is distressed, BP 88/54, HR 115, JVP not visible. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Give an IV crystalloid fluid bolus
questions.explanation: In suspected variceal haemorrhage, vasoactive drugs (e.g., terlipressin) should be started immediately to reduce portal pressure and bleeding, even before endoscopy. Fluid resuscitation may be needed concurrently, but the specific immediate pharmacological intervention is terlipressin. Endoscopy should follow. The Sengstaken tube is reserved for uncontrolled bleeding.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man collapses in the ED waiting room. He has urticaria, wheeze, and is hypotensive (BP 70/40). He was given a painkiller injection 10 minutes ago for a sprained ankle. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Start an IV crystalloid fluid bolus
Place a high-flow oxygen mask at 15 L/min
questions.explanation: Anaphylaxis with shock and respiratory distress is a medical emergency. First-line treatment is intramuscular adrenaline (0.5 mg anterolateral thigh) as per UK Resuscitation Council guidelines. Antihistamines, steroids, and fluids are secondary measures. Oxygen is important but not the single most immediate action.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman on long-term prednisolone for rheumatoid arthritis presents with vomiting, abdominal pain, and confusion. Observations: BP 82/48, HR 110, JVP collapsed, skin hyperpigmentation. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Take blood cultures and start IV antibiotics
Give an IV crystalloid fluid bolus
questions.explanation: Suspected adrenal crisis due to steroid withdrawal requires immediate hydrocortisone replacement. While fluids are also needed, the specific life-saving drug is IV hydrocortisone. Antibiotics may be indicated if infection is precipitant, but the priority is steroid administration. Checking cortisol delays treatment.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of lung cancer presents with breathlessness and hypotension 2 weeks after starting chemotherapy. Observations: BP 82/48, HR 115, JVP elevated, muffled heart sounds, trachea central. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Give an IV crystalloid fluid bolus
questions.explanation: Tamponade is suspected (Beck's triad: hypotension, raised JVP, muffled heart sounds). The immediate step to confirm the diagnosis is bedside echocardiography. Fluids may be transiently helpful but are not diagnostic or definitive. Diuretics are contraindicated (low filling pressures), and CT or balloon pump are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents to the emergency department with confusion, fever, and a blood pressure of 85/50 mmHg, heart rate 110 bpm. Which single finding indicates the need for immediate escalation to critical care?
questions.explanation: A serum lactate >4 mmol/L is a key red flag in sepsis, indicating tissue hypoperfusion and predicting increased mortality, triggering escalation to critical care. While other abnormalities (e.g., oliguria) are concerning, lactate >4 mmol/L is a specific threshold for immediate referral.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman develops sudden urticaria, wheeze, and a blood pressure of 70/40 mmHg after receiving intravenous antibiotics. Which clinical feature indicates that this is immediately life-threatening?
questions.explanation: Stridor indicates laryngeal oedema, which is an immediate threat to the airway and the hallmark of severe anaphylaxis. While hypotension and wheeze are also serious, stridor demands urgent airway intervention.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a road traffic collision. His blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg, heart rate 140 bpm, and he is cool and clammy. Which laboratory finding most strongly indicates the need for massive transfusion?
questions.explanation: A base deficit ≤ -6 mmol/L reflects severe metabolic acidosis due to hypoperfusion and is a key trigger for massive transfusion in trauma. Haemoglobin may be misleadingly normal in acute haemorrhage, and coagulopathy is a later sign.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with metastatic lung cancer presents with dyspnoea, hypotension (90/60 mmHg), and distended neck veins. Which physical sign is most specific for cardiac tamponade?
questions.explanation: Pulsus paradoxus (a fall in systolic BP >10 mmHg on inspiration) is highly specific for cardiac tamponade due to impaired diastolic filling. Muffled heart sounds and Kussmaul sign can occur in other conditions, but pulsus paradoxus is the key discriminatory finding.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman on antihypertensives is found to have a lying blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg and a standing blood pressure of 85/50 mmHg. Which single finding indicates dangerous postural hypotension?
options: Blood pressure difference between arms of 10 mmHg
Heart rate increase of 15 bpm on standing
Fall in diastolic BP of 10 mmHg on standing
Fall in systolic BP of 30 mmHg on standing
questions.explanation: A fall in systolic BP >20 mmHg (here 30 mmHg) on standing is the threshold for significant postural hypotension, associated with syncope and falls. While symptoms like dizziness are relevant, the objective drop of >20 mmHg defines the danger.
